front: What are Architecture Decisions in IT Capacity Planning?
back: Strategic choices that define how IT infrastructure will be structured, deployed, and managed to meet current and future business demands, including hardware, software, network configurations, and deployment models.

front: What is the primary goal of architecture decisions in capacity planning?
back: To ensure optimal performance, scalability, and cost-effectiveness while meeting business requirements.

front: What factors influence hardware selection decisions?
back: Processing power requirements, memory capacity, storage needs, I/O throughput, and anticipated workload growth.
The ability of a system to handle growing amounts of work by adding resources, either by scaling up (vertical) or scaling out (horizontal).

front: What is the difference between Vertical and Horizontal Scaling?
back: Vertical scaling adds power to existing machines (more CPU/RAM); Horizontal scaling adds more machines to distribute the load.

front: What are the key considerations in network architecture decisions?
back: Bandwidth requirements, latency tolerance, network topology, redundancy, and security requirements.
Presentation layer (UI), Application layer (business logic), and Data layer (database) - the standard client-server model.

front: What is the difference between On-Premise and Cloud deployment models?
back: On-premise: owned and managed locally with upfront capital expense; Cloud: rented from providers with operational expense model and elastic scalability.

front: What is Load Balancing in architecture decisions?
back: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed, improving reliability and performance.
States that a distributed system can only guarantee two of three: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance - must sacrifice one.

front: What are the key cost factors in architecture decisions?
back: Capital expenditure (CapEx), operational expenditure (OpEx), licensing costs, maintenance, and energy consumption.
Strategies to ensure critical business functions continue during and after a disaster, including backup systems and failover mechanisms.

front: What is RTO and RPO in capacity planning?
back: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) - max acceptable downtime; RPO (Recovery Point Objective) - max acceptable data loss measured in time.
An approach where applications are built as a collection of small, independent services that communicate over APIs, enabling modular scaling.

front: What is the role of Monitoring in capacity planning architecture?
back: Continuous tracking of system performance, resource utilization, and capacity thresholds to enable proactive scaling and issue detection.
